graph_vector_fulltext.message:
Recital (56) indicates that AI systems used in education or vocational training are (at least in particular) high-risk where they are used for determining access or admission, or for assigning persons to educational/vocational institutions or programmes. Recital (53) adds an exception logic: even if an AI system falls within one of the pre-defined/listed high-risk areas (i.e., a listed domain), there can be specific cases where systems in those areas “do not lead to a significant [risk]” (the excerpt is truncated, but it clearly signals a carve-out based on the system not creating significant risk).

Applied to education: an educational AI system operating in the education domain would *not* be high-risk if, in that specific case, it does not lead to a significant risk to health and safety or to fundamental rights. In practical terms under the text you provided, the more an educational system affects high-stakes decisions like admission/access or assignment to programmes (Recital (56)), the more it aligns with high-risk; conversely, if it operates in education but does not materially affect such outcomes and does not create significant fundamental-rights/health-and-safety risks, Recital (53)’s exception would be the basis for *not* treating it as high-risk despite being in a listed area.
graph_vector_fulltext.metric_question: Recital (56) classifies certain AI systems in education as high-risk. How do the exception conditions in Recital (53) apply to educational AI systems, and when would an educational AI system NOT be high-risk despite operating in a listed domain?
graph_vector_fulltext.metric_answer:
Recital (56) indicates that AI systems used in education or vocational training are (at least in particular) high-risk where they are used for determining access or admission, or for assigning persons to educational/vocational institutions or programmes. Recital (53) adds an exception logic: even if an AI system falls within one of the pre-defined/listed high-risk areas (i.e., a listed domain), there can be specific cases where systems in those areas “do not lead to a significant [risk]” (the excerpt is truncated, but it clearly signals a carve-out based on the system not creating significant risk).

Applied to education: an educational AI system operating in the education domain would *not* be high-risk if, in that specific case, it does not lead to a significant risk to health and safety or to fundamental rights. In practical terms under the text you provided, the more an educational system affects high-stakes decisions like admission/access or assignment to programmes (Recital (56)), the more it aligns with high-risk; conversely, if it operates in education but does not materially affect such outcomes and does not create significant fundamental-rights/health-and-safety risks, Recital (53)’s exception would be the basis for *not* treating it as high-risk despite being in a listed area.

Content: Text Content:
 market of the high-risk AI systems listed in  an annex to this Regulation, it is important that providers or prospective providers of such systems may also benefit  from a specific regime for testing those systems in real world conditions, without participating in an AI regulatory  sandbox. However, in such cases, taking into account the possible consequences of such testing on individuals, it  should be ensured that appropriate and sufficient guarantees and conditions are introduced by this Regulation for  providers or prospective providers. Such guarantees should include
----
 centres. (56) The deployment of AI systems in education is important to promote high-quality digital education and training and  to allow all learners and teachers to acquire and share the necessary digital skills and competences, including media  literacy, and critical thinking, to take an active part in the economy, society, and in democratic processes. However,  AI systems used in education or vocational training, in particular for determining access or admission, for assigning  persons to educational and vocational training institutions or programmes
----
 not directly covered by the intended purpose and  provided for in the instruction for use may nevertheless be reasonably expected to result from readily predictable  human behaviour in the context of the specific characteristics and use of a particular AI system. Any known or  foreseeable circumstances related to the use of the high-risk AI system in accordance with its intended purpose or  under conditions of reasonably foreseeable misuse, which may lead to risks to the health and safety or fundamental  rights should be included in the instructions for use that are
----
 based on the same  methodology and criteria envisaged also for any future amendments of the list of high-risk AI systems that the  Commission should be empowered to adopt, via delegated acts, to take into account the rapid pace of technological  development, as well as the potential changes in the use of AI systems. (53) It is also important to clarify that there may be specific cases in which AI systems referred to in pre-defined areas  specified in this Regulation do not lead to a significant
----

chunks.text: centres. (56) The deployment of AI systems in education is important to promote high-quality digital education and training and to allow all learners and teachers to acquire and share the necessary digital skills and competences, including media literacy, and critical thinking, to take an active part in the economy, society, and in democratic processes. However, AI systems used in education or vocational training, in particular for determining access or admission, for assigning persons to educational and vocational training institutions or programmes

market of the high-risk AI systems listed in an annex to this Regulation, it is important that providers or prospective providers of such systems may also benefit from a specific regime for testing those systems in real world conditions, without participating in an AI regulatory sandbox. However, in such cases, taking into account the possible consequences of such testing on individuals, it should be ensured that appropriate and sufficient guarantees and conditions are introduced by this Regulation for providers or prospective providers. Such guarantees should include

not directly covered by the intended purpose and provided for in the instruction for use may nevertheless be reasonably expected to result from readily predictable human behaviour in the context of the specific characteristics and use of a particular AI system. Any known or foreseeable circumstances related to the use of the high-risk AI system in accordance with its intended purpose or under conditions of reasonably foreseeable misuse, which may lead to risks to the health and safety or fundamental rights should be included in the instructions for use that are

based on the same methodology and criteria envisaged also for any future amendments of the list of high-risk AI systems that the Commission should be empowered to adopt, via delegated acts, to take into account the rapid pace of technological development, as well as the potential changes in the use of AI systems. (53) It is also important to clarify that there may be specific cases in which AI systems referred to in pre-defined areas specified in this Regulation do not lead to a significant
